title: Find on Line
subtitle: {key:f}, {key:F}, {key:t}, {key:T} — jump to a character without leaving the line.
summary: f{c} jumps to the next {c} on the line; F{c} jumps to the previous one. t{c} stops one character before; T{c} stops one character after. ; and , repeat in the same and opposite directions.

blocks.text: {key:/} is overkill for jumping to a paren on the same line. {key:f}{c} jumps to the *next* {c} without ever leaving the line. {key:F} jumps backward. {key:t} ("till") stops one short. Then {key:;} repeats the jump.

blocks.label: Repeat last f/F/t/T in same direction
Repeat last f/F/t/T in opposite direction

blocks.text: {key:t} is the operator-friendly variant. {key:d}{key:t}{key:)} deletes everything up to the next `)` *without* eating it. {key:d}{key:f}{key:)} eats it too. Choose based on whether you want to keep the delimiter.

blocks.title: Why ; Is So Useful
blocks.text:
{key:f}, {key:F}, {key:t}, {key:T} all stash *two pieces of state*: the last character you searched for, and the direction (or modality — `t` vs `f`). {key:;} replays both. {key:,} replays the character but flips the direction.

So {key:f}{key:,}-then-jump-to-some-other-thing-then-{key:;} re-finds the next comma. You don't have to retype it; the keyboard remembers. This is one of those rare cases where two pieces of remembered state make a one-key motion enormously useful.

This state is **per-window**, not per-buffer. Switching files keeps your last find.
{key:f} only sees the *current line* — it never crosses a newline. For cross-line jumps, use search ({key:/}).

blocks.title: {key:f}/{key:t} are a single-line cursor with replay memory
blocks.text: Find-on-line never crosses lines, and it remembers the last `(direction, target)` pair it executed. {key:;} replays that pair; {key:,} replays it with direction flipped. So {key:f}{key:t}{key:;}{key:;} jumps to the third `t` on the line. The memory slot is per-window and survives normal-mode commands but is reset by mode changes.
